title: Eastern long-necked turtle

infobox.value: (***Chelodina longicollis***), also known [commonly](/wiki/Common_name "Common name") as the **common long-necked turtle** and the **common snake-necked turtle**, is an eastern Australian species of freshwater [snake-necked turtle](/wiki/Chelodina "Chelodina") in the [family](/wiki/Family_(biology) "Family (biology)") [Chelidae](/wiki/Chelidae "Chelidae"). The species inhabits a wide variety of water bodies and is an opportunistic feeder. It is a side-necked turtle ([Pleurodira](/wiki/Pleurodira "Pleurodira")), meaning that it bends its head sideways into its shell rather than pulling it directly back.[[6]](#cite_note-KG90-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Kennett1-7)

[Duméril, A.M.C.](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Marie_Constant_Dum%C3%A9ril "André Marie Constant Duméril"); [Bibron, G.](/wiki/Gabriel_Bibron "Gabriel Bibron") (1835). *Erpétologie Générale ou Histoire Naturelle Complète des Reptiles, Tome Second* [Volume 2]. Paris: Roret. 680 pp. (*Chelodina Novæ-Hollandæ*, pp. 443–445). (in French).

[Gray, J.E.](/wiki/John_Edward_Gray "John Edward Gray") (1856). ["On some New Species of Freshwater Tortoises from North America, Ceylon and Australia"](https://biostor.org/reference/14744). *Annals and Magazine of Natural History, Second Series* **18*

first_paragraph: The **eastern long-necked turtle** (***Chelodina longicollis***), also known [commonly](/wiki/Common_name "Common name") as the **common long-necked turtle** and the **common snake-necked turtle**, is an eastern Australian species of freshwater [snake-necked turtle](/wiki/Chelodina "Chelodina") in the [family](/wiki/Family_(biology) "Family (biology)") [Chelidae](/wiki/Chelidae "Chelidae"). The species inhabits a wide variety of water bodies and is an opportunistic feeder. It is a side-necked turtle ([Pleurodira](/wiki/Pleurodira "Pleurodira")), meaning that it bends its head sideways into its shell rather than pulling it directly back.[[6]](#cite_note-KG90-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Kennett1-7)

The **eastern long-necked turtle** (***Chelodina longicollis***), also known [commonly](/wiki/Common_name "Common name") as the **common long-necked turtle** and the **common snake-necked turtle**, is an eastern Australian species of freshwater [snake-necked turtle](/wiki/Chelodina "Chelodina") in the [family](/wiki/Family_(biology) "Family (biology)") [Chelidae](/wiki/Chelidae "Chelidae"). The species inhabits a wide variety of water bodies and is an opportunistic feeder. It is a side-necked turtle ([Pleurodira](/wiki/Pleurodira "Pleurodira")), meaning that it bends its head sideways into its shell rather than pulling it directly back.[[6]](#cite_note-KG90-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Kennett1-7)

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_long-necked_turtle&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

*Chelodina longicollis* is found throughout southeastern Australia, where it is found west of [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide") ([South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia")) eastwards throughout [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)"), the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory"), and [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), and northwards to the [Fitzroy River](/wiki/Fitzroy_River_(Queensland) "Fitzroy River (Queensland)") of [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"). Where *Chelodina longicollis* comes in contact with *[Chelodina canni](/wiki/Chelodina_canni "Chelodina canni")* the two species freely [hybridise](/wiki/Hybrid_(biology) "Hybrid (biology)"), exhibiting hybrid vigour in the [Styx River](/wiki/Styx_River_(Queensland) "Styx River (Queensland)") Drainage of Queensland.[[7]](#cite_note-Kennett1-7)[[8]](#cite_note-m&t2002-8) *C. longicollis* has been [introduced](/wiki/Introduced_species "Introduced species") to [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania"), likely via the pet trade.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)

The wide distribution of the eastern long-necked turtle has allowed it to tolerate climates that are far colder than those that most other [pleurodire](/wiki/Pleurodira "Pleurodira") turtles occur in, including southerly and high-altitude temperate climates. In 2023, a high-altitude long-necked turtle from the [New England Tablelands](/wiki/New_England_(New_South_Wales) "New England (New South Wales)") was found [brumating](/wiki/Brumation "Brumation") in a pool that had completely frozen for 15 days during the winter, marking the first documented case of a pleurodire turtle overwintering under ice.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_long-necked_turtle&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Description")]

The [carapace](/wiki/Turtle_shell#Carapace "Turtle shell") of *Chelodina longicollis* is generally black in colour, though some may be brown. It is broad and flattened with a deep medial groove. The scutes are edged in black in those individuals with a lighter background colour.[[7]](#cite_note-Kennett1-7) The [plastron](/wiki/Turtle_shell#Plastron "Turtle shell") is also very broad and is cream to yellow in colour with sutures edged in black.[[7]](#cite_note-Kennett1-7) The neck is long and narrow, typical of the subgenus *Chelodina*, and reaches a length of approximately 60% of the carapace length. The neck has numerous small pointed [tubercles](/wiki/Tubercle "Tubercle") and is grey to black in colour dorsally, cream below, as is the narrow head.[[7]](#cite_note-Kennett1-7) Females tend to grow to larger sizes and have deeper bodies. The maximum sizes recorded for females and males vary throughout the geographic range; in river environments of the [Murray River](/wiki/Murray_River "Murray River") it has a straight-line carapace length of 28.2 cm (11.1 in) and 24.9 cm (9.8 in) respectively, whereas in the [Latrobe Valley](/wiki/Latrobe_Valley "Latrobe Valley") it is 21.6 cm (8.5 in) and 18.8 cm (7.4 in) respectively.[[7]](#cite_note-Kennett1-7) It is thought this is linked to productivity of the local environment.[[7]](#cite_note-Kennett1-7)

When it feels threatened, this turtle will emit an offensive-smelling fluid from its [musk glands](/wiki/Musk "Musk"). This trait gives the turtle one of its other common names, "stinker".[[10]](#cite_note-eisn1978-10)

## Diet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_long-necked_turtle&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Diet")]

The eastern long-necked turtle is carnivorous, eating a variety of animals. This includes [insects](/wiki/Insects "Insects"), [worms](/wiki/Worm "Worm"), [tadpoles](/wiki/Tadpoles "Tadpoles"), [frogs](/wiki/Frogs "Frogs"), small [fish](/wiki/Fish "Fish"), [crustaceans](/wiki/Crustaceans "Crustaceans"), [molluscs](/wiki/Molluscs "Molluscs"), [plankton](/wiki/Plankton "Plankton"), and [carrion](/wiki/Carrion "Carrion").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Life cycle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_long-necked_turtle&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Life cycle")]

Females of *Chelodina longicollis* take 10 years to reach maturity.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In early summer, the female will lay between two and ten eggs in the banks of her aquatic habitat. Three to five months later the hatchlings break out of their shells. These young turtles often fall prey to predators such as fish and birds. Females will lay one to three clutches of eggs per year.

## Importance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_long-necked_turtle&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Importance")]

Turtles are an important element of the river [ecosystem](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem") owing to their feeding on dead fish after a [fish kill](/wiki/Fish_kill "Fish kill") event, returning [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia") and [dissolved oxygen](/wiki/Dissolved_oxygen "Dissolved oxygen") to the river much faster than by natural decomposition (3–6 days vs 25–27 days).[[13]](#cite_note-Schremmer2025-13)

## Conservation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_long-necked_turtle&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Conservation")]

The species *Chelodina longicollis* is not listed under the Commonwealth [EPBC Act](/wiki/EPBC_Act "EPBC Act") nor any state conservation laws.[[14]](#cite_note-sprat-14)

In [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia"), [foxes](/wiki/Red_foxes_in_Australia "Red foxes in Australia") predate on the eggs in the turtle nests, and may eat the turtles as well. In addition, [river system regulation](/wiki/River_engineering "River engineering") and [roadkill](/wiki/Roadkill "Roadkill") are also considered threats to freshwater turtles of all kinds in the Murray River. Efforts to protect the species include the use of professional shooters to kill foxes; working with local councils to build nesting islands, and improving monitoring of species. After it was found that freshwater turtle numbers had declined by up to 91 per cent some sections of the Murray, conservation efforts increased, with the [Government of South Australia](/wiki/Government_of_South_Australia "Government of South Australia") allocating $450,000 to help establish the state's first turtle management plan, dubbed the "Together Understanding and Restoring Turtles in our Landscapes and Ecosystems" (TURTLE) project. A management strategy is being developed in collaboration with [First Nations people](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians"), for whom the turtles have great cultural significance, along with environmental experts.[[13]](#cite_note-Schremmer2025-13)
